conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me ask: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 19 and still lives at home with me. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few questions.

Since Alex is 19, Child Benefit eligibility typically depends on whether he's in approved education or training. 

**Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?** (For example, is he studying for A-levels, Scottish Highers, or similar qualifications at school or college?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is! Alex is studying for his A-levels at the local sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. 

**When did Alex turn 19, and is he enrolled to continue his A-level studies beyond his 20th birthday?**

I need to understand the timing because there are age limits for Child Benefit even when a young person is in approved education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I see. Well, Alex turned 19 earlier this year. He's currently in his A-level course, but he'll be finishing before he turns 20 - it's a standard two-year A-level programme and he'll complete it before his 20th birthday.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. Let me just gather a bit more information to complete the assessment.

**Are you responsible for Alex - meaning, does he live with you and are you supporting him financially?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. Alex lives here at home with me, and I support him - I cover his living expenses, food, everything he needs while he's studying.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Do you live in the UK, and is Alex also living in the UK with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, we both live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. One final question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or has someone else claimed it for him recently?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me read the official guidance to confirm the eligibility rules before giving you my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Based on the official guidance I've reviewed and the information you've provided, I can now provide you with my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- Alex is 19 years old and studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, which qualifies as approved education
- Child Benefit can continue for young people under 20 who are in approved education or training
- You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you and you financially support him
- You both live in the UK
- No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for him

Since Alex will complete his A-levels before he turns 20, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for him until he finishes his course or turns 20, whichever comes first.

**Please note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge. However, this doesn't affect your eligibility to claim - you can still claim Child Benefit even if the charge applies.

If you haven't already claimed, you should apply as soon as possible. If you need any help with the application process, you can contact the Child Benefit Office.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 19 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16, but you'll still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Alex is 19 years old and is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education. He will complete his course before turning 20. You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you and you support him financially. You both live in the UK (Manchester), and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Alex meets the age and education requirements (under 20 and in approved education), the claimant is responsible for him (he lives at home and is financially supported), they live in the UK, and no one else is claiming for him.

All eligibility criteria are met.
